ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for ikan masak pedas; mapped to broader 'fish dish' class (FOODON_03301116) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for ikan masak pedas; A0CKP (cooked fish) is the closest defensible broader match.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for ikan masak pedas or equivalent spiced fish curry preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of ikan masak pedas as a side dish alongside rice is approximately 150 g, comprising one or two pieces of fish with sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Ikan masak pedas — Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient data
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for ikan masak pedas (cr_id F03020018). All eight reported nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) are sourced at confidence 0.95. Secondary nutrients not reported by HPB (saturated fat, sugar, potassium, cholesterol, vitamins) are set to -1 per numeric hygiene rules.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for ikan masak pedas. GI estimated at approximately 25 (low) based on analogy with other low-carbohydrate fish-based curry dishes (carbohydrate 2.6 g/100g, predominantly from tamarind and spice paste). The low carbohydrate content means glycaemic impact is negligible. GL per 150 g serving = (25 × 2.6 × 1.5) / 100 ≈ 1.0, confirming very low glycaemic load. Analogous reference: fish curry / spiced fish preparations in University of Sydney GI Database, all classified low GI.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 644 mg/100 g — exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid ikan masak pedas in standard preparation; if consumed, restrict to a small portion (≤75 g) and request reduced-salt or reduced-belacan preparation to bring sodium below 400 mg per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (2.6 g/100 g), moderate protein (11 g/100 g), and dietary fibre (2.1 g/100 g) produce a negligible glycaemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ikan masak pedas is suitable for type 2 diabetes management due to its low carbohydrate content and estimated low GI (~25); monitor total meal glycaemic load by controlling the portion of accompanying rice rather than the fish dish itself.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (644 mg/100 g) is a cardiovascular risk factor, partially offset by fish-derived omega-3 fatty acids and anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease patients, the omega-3 content from fish is beneficial, but the high sodium content (644 mg/100 g) requires portion control and low-sodium preparation; consume no more than 100 g per meal and pair with potassium-rich vegetables to partially mitigate sodium impact.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Fish omega-3 and anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger) are beneficial; chilli and potential nightshade ingredients may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ikan masak pedas may benefit RA patients through fish-derived omega-3 fatty acids and curcumin from turmeric, but individuals sensitive to nightshades (chilli, tomato) should monitor joint response and request preparation without nightshade ingredients where possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ikan masak pedas is a traditional Malay and Mamak fish dish in which whole or portioned fish — commonly mackerel (ikan kembung), snapper, or stingray — is simmered in a bold, tangy, and fiery sauce made from tamarind (asam), dried chillies, turmeric, lemongrass, galangal, shallots, garlic, and belacan (fermented shrimp paste). The dish is a staple of Malaysian and Singaporean Mamak cuisine, served alongside steamed white rice or roti. The sauce is characterised by a deep reddish-orange colour, a sour-spicy flavour profile from tamarind and chilli, and a fragrant base from the rempah (spice paste). Nutritionally, ikan masak pedas provides 109 kcal per 100 g, with 11 g protein, 6.1 g fat, and only 2.6 g carbohydrate, making it a low-carbohydrate, moderate-protein dish. Dietary fibre is 2.1 g/100 g from the spice base. The primary clinical concern is high sodium at 644 mg/100 g, which exceeds safe thresholds for hypertension. The fish component provides omega-3 fatty acids, and turmeric contributes curcumin with anti-inflammatory properties. The dish is suitable for type 2 diabetes management due to its negligible glycaemic load, but should be consumed in controlled portions by individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease.
common_user_queries: Is ikan masak pedas suitable for diabetics?
How much sodium is in ikan masak pedas?
Is ikan masak pedas good for high blood pressure?
What fish is used in ikan masak pedas?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95 for an exact match to ikan masak pedas (cr_id F03020018). Secondary nutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1 per numeric hygiene rules. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database principles given the very low carbohydrate content.
notes: Secondary nutrients not reported by HPB Singapore Foodo for this entry — saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, sugar_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this traditional mixed-ingredient preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or IMR Malaysia MyFCD as of 2026-04-16. No zero-macro justification required as all core macros (protein_g 11.0, fat_g 6.1, carbohydrate_g 2.6) are non-zero. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for ikan masak pedas was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-16. Ontology mapped to broader FoodOn fish dish class and FoodEx2 cooked fish code; no species-specific or preparation-specific codes exist in current ontology releases.
